seasonal_wine.name: Grandi Langhe en primeur, late January
seasonal_wine.description: Piedmont's en primeur equivalent gathers more than 500 Langhe and Roero wineries at the OGR in Turin to pour the newest Barolo, Barbaresco and Roero releases for trade, press and international buyers over two days.
seasonal_wine.tip: Trade and press only; line up appointments with the smaller La Morra and Serralunga estates before the floor fills on the first morning.

seasonal_wine.name: Barolo new-vintage release, spring
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, vintage released after 38 months of ageing
seasonal_wine.description: Barolo DOCG cannot be sold until 1 January of the fourth year after harvest, after at least 38 months ageing. Each January the newest annata reaches the market, with Riserva bottlings following after 62 months.
seasonal_wine.tip: The just-released vintage is tight; ask cellars to also pour a five-year-older bottle so you taste where the wine is heading.

seasonal_wine.name: Vine bud-break in the Moscato hills, spring
seasonal_wine.description: Spring brings bud-break across the Moscato hills of Canelli and Asti, the UNESCO sparkling-wine landscape. The young Moscato d'Asti and Asti Spumante of the previous harvest are at their fresh, grapey peak.
seasonal_wine.tip: Moscato d'Asti is best within a year of harvest; buy the freshest vintage on the shelf and chill it hard.

seasonal_wine.name: Vinum Alba open-air tasting, spring
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late April into early May
seasonal_wine.description: Over two long weekends between April and May the streets and courtyards of Alba become a tasting trail for Langhe, Roero and Monferrato wines, with workshops, cellar visits and street food alongside the pours.
seasonal_wine.tip: Buy the tasting carnet online during the early-booking window before mid-April for the lower price.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering in the Langhe vineyards, early summer
seasonal_wine.description: June flowering sets the size of the coming crop across the Barolo and Barbaresco hills. It is the quietest visiting window of the year, with the UNESCO vineyards in full green and cellars open for unhurried tastings.
seasonal_wine.tip: June is shoulder season; estates that need months of notice in October will often take a tasting booking with a few days' warning now.

seasonal_wine.name: Collisioni festival in Alba, July
seasonal_wine.description: The Collisioni agri-rock festival fills the UNESCO Langhe with music, literature and wine across July, with daytime tastings and wine-grower meetings framed by the Barolo and Barbaresco hills.
seasonal_wine.tip: Concert nights book early; the daytime food-and-wine programme is the reason to come for tasters rather than gig-goers.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison and ripening, August
seasonal_wine.description: August is veraison, when Nebbiolo, Barbera and Dolcetto change colour and begin their final ripening. Growers thin the canopy and green-harvest, and the hills empty for the Italian summer holiday.
seasonal_wine.tip: Many cellars close in mid-August for the Ferragosto break; confirm opening before you drive out to a small estate.

seasonal_wine.name: Douja d'Or wine fair in Asti, September
seasonal_wine.description: Asti's national wine competition and fair pours award-winning Italian wines across the historic centre each September, with Asti DOCG, Barbera and Monferrato reds taking centre stage in Piazza San Secondo.
seasonal_wine.tip: The Douja d'Or competition seal on a back label is a reliable shortcut to a well-made bottle when shopping Monferrato wines.

seasonal_wine.name: Dolcetto and Barbera harvest, September
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest opens in September with early-ripening Dolcetto, followed by Barbera. Cellars hum with fermentation while the Nebbiolo waits for October, and visitors can watch the year's first crush.
seasonal_wine.tip: Ask whether you can taste fermenting must straight from the tank; many small La Morra and Dogliani growers will oblige during the crush.

seasonal_wine.name: Nebbiolo harvest, October
seasonal_wine.description: The late-ripening Nebbiolo that becomes Barolo and Barbaresco is picked in October, often under the autumn fog that gives the grape its name. It is the most atmospheric and most booked-out month in the Langhe.
seasonal_wine.tip: Book estate visits and restaurant tables weeks ahead; October in the Langhe is peak season and beds sell out across Alba and La Morra.

seasonal_wine.name: Alba White Truffle Fair, October to December
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, weekends mid October to early December
seasonal_wine.description: The International Alba White Truffle Fair runs across autumn weekends, pairing the prized tuber with Barolo, Barbaresco and Nebbiolo in the natural high point of the Langhe wine-and-food calendar.
seasonal_wine.tip: White truffle and aged Barolo is the region's signature match; eat the truffle shaved over plain tajarin so the wine stays the star.

seasonal_wine.name: Bagna cauda and young-wine season, late autumn into winter
seasonal_wine.description: As the cold sets in, Piedmont tables turn to bagna cauda, the warm anchovy-and-garlic dip, washed down with young Dolcetto and Barbera. The year's first new wines pour at village sagre across the Langhe and Monferrato.
seasonal_wine.tip: Bagna cauda calls for a fruity young red, not a tannic Barolo; reach for Dolcetto or a light Grignolino instead.
